attributes.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for a routine health check. She works as a trauma surgeon in a busy metropolitan hospital. During the consultation, she mentions that she chose surgery as a career after witnessing her younger sister die in a car accident when she was 16 years old. She explains that the experience made her want to 'cut into things and fix them.' She finds her work deeply satisfying despite its demanding nature. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following ego defence mechanisms best describes this patient's psychological adaptation?
attributes.explanation:
This case illustrates sublimation, where potentially destructive impulses (in this case, aggressive or destructive urges following trauma) are channelled into socially acceptable and productive activities (becoming a surgeon). Sublimation is considered one of the most mature defence mechanisms as it results in constructive outcomes while allowing for some expression of the underlying impulse.

Reaction formation involves converting an unacceptable impulse into its opposite. For example, expressing excessive love for someone you actually resent. This is not demonstrated here as the patient is not converting her impulses into their opposite but rather redirecting them constructively.

Displacement involves redirecting feelings from their original target to a less threatening substitute. For example, someone who is angry at their boss might come home and kick their dog. The patient is not simply redirecting her feelings to a different target but transforming them into productive work.

Intellectualisation involves dealing with emotional conflict by excessive abstract thinking while minimising feelings. While surgeons may use intellectualisation in their work, this case specifically demonstrates the transformation of impulses rather than their suppression through abstract thinking.

Compensation involves making up for real or perceived deficits by emphasising other qualities. This defence mechanism is not illustrated here as the patient is not trying to make up for a deficit but rather channelling specific impulses into productive work.

Understanding defence mechanisms is crucial for clinicians as it helps them recognise and support healthy psychological adaptation. Sublimation is particularly valuable as it allows for psychological growth while contributing positively to society.
learningPoints: Sublimation is an ego defence mechanism where socially unacceptable impulses are channelled into socially acceptable activities.
Sublimation is considered a mature defence mechanism as it results in productive outcomes.
Recognition of healthy defence mechanisms helps clinicians support adaptive coping strategies.
references: Sadock BJ, Sadock VA, Ruiz P. Kaplan and Sadock's comprehensive textbook of psychiatry. lippincott Williams & wilkins; 2017 May 11.

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
